summary: Gruber excoriates France's competition authority for fining Apple €150 million over App Tracking Transparency, arguing the decision sides with surveillance advertisers against users. He contends that ATT's simple yes/no prompt actually accomplished what EU privacy laws only pretend to do, as proven by Meta losing billions in ad revenue. The French regulator's complaint that two layers of consent is excessive is, in Gruber's view, really an objection that the effective layer (ATT) should be removed while the ineffective one (EU cookie consent) remains. He frames the fine as the result of advertising industry lobbying, noting that the groups who complained are precisely those whose tracking ATT successfully curtailed. Gruber argues EU privacy regulations bury users in confusion by design, benefiting the ad industry, while ATT's plain-language consent actually empowers users.
key_points: ATT demonstrably reduced cross-app tracking — Meta lost billions — while EU/French privacy laws had no measurable effect on the surveillance ad industry
France's fine was triggered by advertising industry complaints, not user complaints, revealing whose interests the decision actually serves
The regulator's logic that two consent layers is 'excessive' conveniently targets the effective consent mechanism while preserving the toothless one
EU cookie consent dialogs are deliberately confusing and benefit advertisers, while ATT's simple yes/no prompt is clear and user-friendly
The decision reflects a bureaucratic worldview where process matters more than outcomes — France is punishing Apple for actually accomplishing what EU privacy law only pretends to achieve
money_quotes: ATT has had measurable effects because users understand it, and they prefer not to be tracked. EU and French privacy laws are largely ineffective because, in practice, they bury users with confusion.

What the Autorité de la Concurrence is saying, in so many words, is that two layers of consent is too much, and the only one that's necessary is the one that advertising lobbying groups don't object to, not the one they do (but which users understand and like).
App Tracking Transparency actually accomplished, in practice, via user-focused plain-language consent, what the EU's privacy laws were intended to do but do not.
This fine boils down to France declaring that Apple shouldn't have actually done what the EU was pretending to do.

It's like a consortium of sketchy pawn shops complaining to the authorities after a popular retailer successfully cracked down on an organized shoplifting/pickpocketing ring, and the authorities then fining the retailer for the damage to the pawnbrokers' business fencing stolen goods — and for exposing the police as ineffective.
The bureaucratic hurdles they impose are to the benefit, not detriment, of the surveillance ad industry.

They're acting at the behest of the very developers and advertising companies who were (and still are) trying to conduct cross-app tracking that App Tracking Transparency successfully gave users some control over.
key_insight: France fined Apple for building the privacy protection that actually works, at the behest of the advertisers whose tracking it successfully disrupted, while preserving the EU's own ineffective privacy theater.
